We haven't done the diagnostic work to find out what benefits they're looking for, what hot button benefits are they looking for, so we could apply those in our presentation. And by the way, there's a big disconnect often. You took this out of the context of offer now, right? Yep. You're not talking about features and benefits of the offer, you're just talking about features and benefits in general. So you will constantly see, because salespeople are not good at it at this, or not managed so that they are good at it. You'll see a big disconnect between what's going on in the advertising and what's going on on the sales floor. So the advertising people will actually get this right at the front end of this whole process. So you'll see a car commercial, or a series of car commercials, that is all about being the parent of a teenager that that teenager is just getting their driver's license. And you having picked the car because it thinks for itself and it protects this kid and you can actually hand the keys to the kid and go in the house and watch the game and not, you know, be by the window waiting for the sound of the crash. So they'll get this right. But then to his point, if you now get to the car dealership and you get with the car salesman, there's a complete disconnect that has happened along the way. And the car salesman will not pick up on that conversation. He will not refer to that conversation. He won't be emotionalizing. He'll be talking about how that it's the airbags. Now, he's in the same place, but he's defaulted down to features, right? The things got six airbags. Honda's DumaFludge has only got four. And ours deploy with six pounds less impact. And so he's going to have an airbag conversation. And the customer isn't even sophisticated enough to appreciate the conversation. I mean, who knows, right? But the disconnect occurs. So often the advertising people will get it right. They will be in the benefit territory. And it will disintegrate down to feature territory at the selling end. You know, one of my first people at Tommy about selling when I was in radio, she says, whenever you name a feature, because sometimes you do have to talk about the feature, by the way. I think a lot of people say, well, you never talk. I've read that in sales books. Don't talk about features at all. Well, which is wrong, because it goes back to the emotional logic that we talked about in the last segment. What she said was whenever you say a feature, though, add, which means to you. And I just thought, or you don't have to verbally say it every single time. But if you think about it, so you say what's the feature. So this has six airbags, which means to you, then you automatically get to the benefit. I also think that, you know, we don't want to downplay features, because in some areas, features are important, the specs. You know, if you're selling FDA regulated, or if you're selling in healthcare, you're selling in computers, you know, there's going to be somewhere along the line where there needs to be a matchup of the features to make sure that it's compatible with what the objectives of the organization are, whether that's a computer program that has to talk to another computer program, or it has to be regulated by the FDA. So the specs in some sense matters. But the pros will focus on the features. The outcome of the surgery, not the thing of a jigger bobs that caused the surgery to happen, you know, within the OR. See, even in those cases, there is, so you will have B2B people raise this B2B as different than consumer. Even in the environments that he just discussed, the tendency is now to heavily weight fact statistics. But there's almost an assumption on the part of the buyer in those environments that you are meeting the minimum ante. Nobody really from the, so we'll use your FDA example. So nobody is really going to be sitting across from the buyer of a nutritional supplement company, selling their labeling and packaging services. Who does not, the buyers go to assume by the very fact that they are there and that they do labeling and packaging for nutritional supplements, that they meet the minimum standards of the FDA. Right? Right. I have a client I'm working with right now, the state of Oklahoma, to get business system moved to Oklahoma. So.
